response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As someone who spends a lot of time on social media, I've noticed how fake content can really mess with your head. It's like seeing these perfect lives that don't exist—I'm constantly scrolling through feeds filled with unrealistic beauty standards and exaggerated stories.

I have anxiety from my personality traits, which makes me extra sensitive to this kind of thing. Seeing all this fake stuff just amplifies my own worries about not being good enough or measuring up. It's a vicious cycle that can be hard to break out of, especially when I'm trying to stay connected with friends and family back home in Chicago.

But since I have a master's degree and am self-employed running my cleaning business, I've learned some coping mechanisms over the years. One thing that helps is curating my own feed by unfollowing accounts that make me feel inadequate or overwhelmed. It feels selfish at times because social media was originally meant as a way to share life with others.

However, being in an Islamic faith gives me perspective on not getting too caught up in comparisons during prayer and reflection time each day. And my moderate conscientiousness helps me stay organized so that I don't spend too much time mindlessly scrolling when I could be focusing on real tasks like managing client schedules or promoting my business.

I think the key is finding balance between being informed about what's happening in the world through social media versus getting sucked into this endless comparison vortex. For example, sometimes I use cycling as a way to clear my head before returning to my phone.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Step-by-step reasoning: First, consider how fake content affects individuals with anxiety and high neuroticism scores (as per Open Ocean traits). Then, connect personal experiences of daily social media usage in Chicago during an Islamic spiritual practice. Next, incorporate professional background as a self-employed cleaner who needs to manage time effectively but also deal with emotional triggers from online content.
